channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Affective Looping
item.description: Affective looping is the temporal structure in which an artifact repeats and cycles in order to hold the viewer in a feeling-state rather than advancing them through a narrative arc. Where conventional narrative organizes time as progression — setup, complication, resolution — affective looping organizes time as repetition with small variations. The viewer does not move through; the viewer stays in. The point of the form is not to deliver an arc but to sustain a mood.

item.title: Consequence-Free Violence
item.description: Consequence-free violence is a specific mechanism within an artifact’s moral physics: visually maximal harm staged with zero narrative residue. A character is hit, exploded, dismembered, beaten, or killed; the next scene proceeds as if it did not happen. The harm is not hidden or aestheticized; it is fully shown, often elaborately so. What is suppressed is not the visibility of the violence but its persistence — its capacity to alter the world that follows from it.

item.title: Memetic Warfare
item.description:
Memetic warfare is the use of meme-form content — short, recombinable, emotionally-loaded units circulated through algorithmic platforms — as tactical influence. The phrase originates in late-2010s U.S. and NATO doctrinal discussion, where it names a recognized capability area within information operations: the production and distribution of low-cost, high-virality content designed to shape perception, recruit attention, harass adversaries, or shift the affective register of an information environment.

Within cybernetic postliberalism, memetic warfare is treated less as a novel doctrine than as the open avowal of a longer state-aesthetic capacity. Memes did not invent recombinable, emotionally-loaded, low-cost content; advertising, propaganda, and entertainment had been producing it for a century. What memes added was the platformal substrate: an algorithmic distribution system that made meme-form content the dominant unit of public attention. Memetic warfare names what happens when state actors operate inside that substrate using the same forms its native users have developed.

item.title: Moral Displacement
item.description: Moral displacement is the operation by which an artifact reroutes the locus of moral concern from structural conditions onto individual character. A war film acknowledges that soldiers were sent into a meaningless campaign and asks, instead, whether the protagonist personally found meaning. A wellness app acknowledges that workers are exhausted by a system that extracts them and asks, instead, whether they have built a personal sustainable practice. A recruitment video acknowledges that influence operations exist and asks, instead, whether you are clever enough to join them. In each case, a structural fact is named and then displaced: the moral question the structure raises is converted into a moral question about a person.

item.title: Moral Physics
item.description: Moral physics is the implicit rules a media artifact establishes for how harm, responsibility, and consequence work inside the world it depicts. Where genre tells the audience what kind of story they are watching, moral physics tells the audience what kind of universe the story occurs in: whether bodies break, whether choices accumulate, whether catastrophe leaves residue, whether cause links to effect. These rules are rarely declared. They are taught through repetition — the way a body bounces back, the way a death is or is not registered, the way the next scene does or does not remember the last.

item.title: Rubber-Hose Realism
item.description: Rubber-hose realism is a moral-physics regime in which bodies are elastic, harm is reversible, and the world resets between scenes. It takes its name from the rubber-hose animation style of the 1920s and 1930s — Fleischer’s Out of the Inkwell, the early black-and-white Mickey Mouse — in which characters’ limbs bent without joints and their bodies could be stretched, flattened, dismembered, and reassembled within seconds. The technique is the regime’s origin; the regime itself is broader.

item.title: Soft Surrealism
item.description: Soft surrealism is the contemporary visual surface in which collage, glitch, screen-within-screen, archival fragment, retro-cartoon revival, distorted voiceover, and symbolic juxtaposition operate together to produce a sense that reality itself is malleable. Where historical surrealism aimed to disrupt bourgeois common sense by yoking incompatible images, soft surrealism aims, in its state and brand uses, to establish the impression of authorship over the real: the feeling that whoever made this content has access to a level above the rest of the information environment, can manipulate its layers, and is offering the viewer a glimpse from inside that authorship.

item.title: State-Aesthetic Continuum
item.description: The state-aesthetic continuum is the single apparatus, varying by institutional cover rather than by formal vocabulary, in which commercial entertainment, corporate soft power, brand-justice marketing, platform-native creator content, recruitment media, and overt psychological operations produce affectively-shaped audiences. The term names what these registers share — a common moral-physics regime, a common library of formal techniques, a common audience pool — and treats their differences as differences of position on the continuum rather than as differences of category.

item.title: Strategic Infantilization
item.description: Strategic infantilization is the operation by which an artifact addresses an adult audience in a register associated with children’s media so that the audience’s critical distance is reduced before content is delivered. The mechanism is not about depicting children, talking about children, or making content for children. It is about speaking the way a children’s medium speaks — using the formal vocabulary of address that audiences first learned to receive as children — even when the audience and the stakes are adult.

item.title: Anarchist grammar
item.description: Anarchist grammar is the rhetorical sequence that anarchist political speech follows regardless of its surface content — a six-step structure that mirror-inverts the corresponding steps of fascist grammar. Where fascist grammar declares crisis, names a pure community, denounces enemies, reframes suffering as sacrifice, promises destiny, and asserts inevitability, anarchist grammar refuses each move with a structurally specific countermove. The framework’s analysis is that the inversion does not constitute an exit from the operational closure within which fascist grammar operates; anarchist grammar inhabits the same metabolism as another available genre.

item.title: Attributing
item.description: To attribute is to assign a property, identity, or condition to a being or situation. The act looks descriptive — she is depressed; they are racist; this is trauma; that is collapse — and the framework’s claim is that under ontogenring the act is not descriptive but constitutive. Attributing installs the named being or situation into a genre-formatted ontology, with the ontogenric entitlements and anticipations that follow from the installation arriving with it.

item.title: Californian projection
item.description: Californian projection is the formation in which California — the actual coastal territory and the imaginative screen of the same name — has served as the historical laboratory in which the apparatus iterates. The Misiones Californianas, the Gold Rush, Hollywood, Esalen, Silicon Valley, the wellness industry: each is a stage of one continuous apparatus, not a separate phenomenon. The land is not California; California is what we make of it. The projection is the apparatus.

item.title: Collapse as narrativization
item.description:
Collapse as narrativization is the result of ontogenring acting on destabilization. The structural condition produced by saturation does not arrive at subjects as a structural condition; it arrives genre-formatted, with a name (collapse), an affective register (dread, mourning, resolve), a temporal shape (decline, fall, possibility-of-renewal), and a set of subject-positions ready to occupy each phase (the prepper, the doomer, the chronicler, the mourner, the rebuilder).

¶Etymology and discursive history

The word collapse derives from Latin collabi — col- (together) + labi (to slip, fall) — to fall together, to fall in one body. The medieval and early-modern uses are physical and mechanical: a structure collapses; a cave collapses; a body collapses. The civilizational sense — collapse as the structural breakdown of a society — emerges in the eighteenth century with Gibbon’s account of Rome, but does not become a popular discursive formation until the twentieth.

item.title: Crisis-settlerism
item.description: Crisis-settlerism is the intensification of settlerism under conditions of structural saturation and destabilization. When the apparatus’s contradictions become visible, settler subject-positions are reproduced more aggressively, more aestheticized, and more violent. The legitimating erasures that operate quietly in ordinary settlerism — terra nullius, the Doctrine of Discovery, the frontier myth — shift toward overt assertion of the right to displace. The formation does not invent itself in crisis; it is settlerism’s pre-existing structure operating under conditions where its work is no longer concealable.

item.title: Destabilization
item.description:
Destabilization is what saturation induces. The system loses the prior equilibrium that absorbed its constitutive contradictions; the routings that previously bridged the gap between promise and production stop converging; what had been routine — the daily reproductions of subjects, infrastructures, expectations — begins to register as crisis. Destabilization is not a single event; it is the new ambient condition in which events are encountered.

¶Lineage and adjacent traditions

The underlying dynamics have been studied across disciplines, and destabilization in the framework’s sense draws from each.

item.title: Expert naïveté
item.description: Expert naïveté is the formation in which a person publicly recognized as expert in one domain, induced into novel learning by crisis, performs expertise on adjacent topics they have only just begun to learn — with the audience reading the new material from the prior expert position rather than from the actual depth of understanding behind it. The mismatch is the formation’s signature: the audience attributes a depth that the producer does not in fact have, and the producer’s own framing typically does not correct the misattribution.

item.title: Liberal ontologizing
item.description:
Liberal ontologizing is the foundational operation of liberal thought. It treats the things of the world — persons, peoples, properties, values — as substances with inherent attributes, existing prior to and independently of the relations they participate in. The world that liberal ontologizing produces is a world of pre-political individuals, pre-relational entities, sovereign units that then enter into the social, the political, and the economic.

The operation is not a description of how things actually are; it is a metaphysical posture that has the appearance of being a neutral default. To denaturalize it is the first move of any analysis that does not take its products for granted.

item.title: Liberalism legitimacy crisis
item.description: The Liberalism Legitimacy Crisis is the named structural moment in which liberalism’s constitutive contradictions become experientially insoluble for its subjects rather than locally manageable, and the system shifts from promising resolution of those contradictions to promising regulation of the symptoms. The system loses legitimacy as an ideological system in the specific sense that it can no longer produce the resolutions it has historically promised; what it can still produce is administrative management.

item.title: Ontogenring
item.description: Ontogenring is the process by which subjects are produced through genre-formatting. Where genring reduces a living people to a fixed literary type — selecting, codifying, and substituting features until the type replaces the people — ontogenring extends the same operation up one level, to the production of subjects themselves. Beings are no longer merely placed in genres; their being is constituted by the genre. The genre is the ontology. Hence the onto- prefix: it is not only that experiences are generic, it is that what one is is generic.

item.title: Platformal liberalism
item.description: Platformal liberalism is the operating mode of liberalism in which platforms — digital platforms primarily, but also platform-style logics that have spread beyond software — become the primary medium of subject-formation, contradiction-management, and the distribution of feeling rules. Liberalism’s central operations are no longer carried out by states, parties, churches, or schools as the dominant institutions; they are carried out by platforms, with the older institutions reorganizing themselves around platform logics in order to remain operative.

item.title: Saturation
item.description: Saturation is the structural condition in which a system can no longer absorb additional load without strain. The variety of disturbances arriving from the system’s environment has begun to exceed the variety of internal states the system can use to absorb them. Under unsaturated conditions, the system extends — adapts internal variety to match external variety, expands its repertoire, defers what it cannot immediately resolve. Under saturation, the extending stops working; the system is receiving more than it can metabolize.

item.title: Settlerism
item.description: Settlerism is the structural formation of governance organized around continuous displacement-and-replacement of indigenous relations, the production of stable settler subject-positions on a continuously-renewed colonial frontier, and the legal-cultural infrastructures that legitimate the displacement by erasing indigenous prior presence. It is a structure rather than an event, a regime rather than an episode — settler arrival is not the founding moment of the formation but its first iteration, with each subsequent generation continuing the displacement under different vocabularies.

item.title: Genre Coherence
item.description: Genre coherence is the demand that a nation’s cast of folk types remain stable. Once a nation imagines itself as a collection of authentic genre figures — the Noble Indian, the Hearty Bavarian, the Mountain Kurd, the salt-of-the-earth farmer — the integrity of the national story depends on the characters remaining as assigned. Anyone who does not fit the genre becomes a problem for the story. Not necessarily an ideological enemy, but a narrative disruption: a character who breaks the fourth wall.

item.title: Genring
item.description: Genring is the reduction of a living people to a fixed literary type. It is not a metaphor for stereotyping but a specific political operation with three steps: selection (extracting visually striking, emotionally resonant features from a people’s life — dress, diet, habitat, ritual), codification (assembling those features into a stable character template and circulating it through novels, performances, images, and festivals until it becomes self-reinforcing), and substitution (the template replaces the people, so that encountering the people means encountering the character).

item.title: Temporal Middle
item.description:
The temporal middle is the position of inheriting, continuing, and transforming a tradition without either renewing it wholesale or betraying it. Every living person occupies this position. No one is purely “traditional” (frozen in the past) or purely “modern” (severed from the past). People inherit what they inherit, transform what they can, and continue.

The concept names what settler colonialism, genring, and binary nationalist frameworks systematically eliminate. The elimination operates by offering only two positions: the traditional subject (who matches the assigned genre — the Noble Indian, the Hearty Bavarian, the Mountain Kurd) and the assimilated subject (who has abandoned the genre and joined the dominant culture). There is no position for someone who is doing what every living culture does: inheriting, engaging, adapting, continuing.

item.title: Genre calibration
item.description: A person loses their job during a recession. In the self-help genre, this is a “pivot” — an opportunity to reinvent yourself. In the activist genre, it is evidence of systemic exploitation. In the therapeutic genre, it is a wound requiring healing. The recession is the same recession. The job loss is the same job loss. What changes is the genre — the set of expectations determining what the event means, what feelings are appropriate, and what counts as resolution.

item.title: Californication
item.description: Californication is the process by which liberal governance sustains itself under structural contradiction by formatting crisis as personally manageable affect. The term names what happens when a political system cannot resolve its own contradictions but can teach its subjects to experience those contradictions as personal conditions – as mood, trauma, growth opportunity, or lifestyle. The subject who meditates through a housing crisis, journals through a pandemic, or microdoses through ecological collapse is not failing to respond politically. They are doing exactly what the system requires: converting structural incoherence into self-regulatory behavior.

item.title: Fascist grammar
item.description:
In July 1932, Joseph Goebbels told a crowd of Germans that their nation was collapsing, that the Weimar system was in ruins, that parasites and traitors had corrupted the community, that years of humiliation would be redeemed through struggle, that the German people’s destiny was assured, and that the storm would come and sweep away the old order.

In September 2025, Stephen Miller stood before tens of thousands in Arizona and declared that enemies had tried to kill Charlie Kirk and made him immortal, that “we are the ones who build” while the enemies “create nothing,” that the movement’s lineage stretched from Athens to Rome to Monticello, that “the light will defeat the dark,” and that “we are the storm.”

item.title: Harm governance
item.description: Someone shares a small act of direct action online — stealing banner materials, organizing unpermitted aid, refusing to comply with public health abandonment. The immediate response is not about the target of the action or its consequences. It is about the terms under which it was shared. Was it safe to post? Will someone misinterpret it? Did the actor properly disclose the risk? The pushback is not strategic critique from within an organizing context. It is supervision — the internalized logic of an administrative state, reproduced between people who no longer trust the state itself.

item.title: Industrial intellectualism
item.description: A scholar writes about an abolitionist movement. The movement failed — the facility it opposed was built and is operating. But the paper succeeds. It moves through the correct arc: death-world (thesis), life-worlding (antithesis), building-and-fighting (synthesis). The citations confirm genre membership. The analysis produces the satisfaction the genre demands. The paper circulates, gets cited, gets taught. The facility continues operating. The paper does not need the facility to have been stopped. It needs the narrative to resolve correctly.

item.title: Neurotic platformal intellectual
item.description: Someone posts a detailed analysis of monetary policy on a social media platform. Within hours, people with no economics training are debating the analysis in replies — not asking questions but offering corrections, alternative theories, and confident disagreements. No one does this with surgery. When a surgeon posts about a procedure, non-surgeons do not show up to debate incision technique. But policy analysis on a platform is discourse, and discourse is everyone’s medium. The platform makes no structural distinction between someone who has spent years studying monetary systems and someone who read a thread about inflation that morning. What it registers is engagement.

item.title: Savior-slave subject
item.description: A person sorts their recycling, votes in every election, stays informed about geopolitics, practices mindfulness, donates to mutual aid funds, checks their privilege, buys local, and advocates for systemic change on social media. They are exhausted. They are told — and they believe — that this exhaustion is what responsibility feels like. The systems producing the crises they manage are not accessible to any of these actions. They know this. They continue.

item.title: Zen fascism
item.description: In 1979, Jello Biafra of the Dead Kennedys wrote “California Uber Alles,” a song that identified something no political theorist had yet named: a mode of governance in which serenity is compulsory. The target was Jerry Brown’s California — a political culture that had fused counterculture spirituality with administrative power, producing a regime where the well-governed subject was the calm subject. Not compliant through fear, but compliant through inner peace. The song’s title made the connection explicit: this was authoritarianism, but it smelled like yoga.

item.title: Coherent confusion
item.description:
After the April 2025 tariff announcement, commentators produced thousands of explanations: it was a negotiating tactic, a policy error, a deliberate shock, a market test, a distraction. These explanations contradicted each other, yet each felt plausible. People remained confused — but they kept interpreting, kept producing takes, kept engaging. The system did not need them to understand. It needed them to keep trying.

Coherent confusion names this governance condition: subjects confused but functional, unable to form a stable interpretation of events yet still participating in the systems that produce the confusion. The confusion is not a failure of governance. The confusion is governance. The interpretive labor of trying to make sense of contradictory signals — the takes, the threads, the analyses, the commentary — is itself what sustains system coherence.

item.title: Socially ontogenic media
item.description: A surgeon in a war zone cracks a joke while amputating a leg. The audience laughs — not because it is funny, but because M*A*S*H has spent seasons teaching them that laughter is how you survive a system that processes bodies without pause. The show does not explain how to cope with war. It does not argue a position on war. It models a way of being inside war’s contradictions: ironic, compassionate, rhythmically attuned to crisis that never resolves. The viewer does not learn a lesson. They internalize a posture.
